summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Microsoft's Calculator Plus for defaulting to an ugly custom skin instead of the standard Windows UI. While acknowledging that skinning is somewhat subjective, he argues that non-standard interfaces need strong justification and professional design. He contrasts this poorly-executed skin with Windows Media Center, which has a legitimate reason (couch usage with remote) for its custom interface. The post advocates for respecting user expectations around standard OS look and feel.
key_points: Custom UI skins should not be the default, especially when poorly designed
Deviating from standard OS look and feel requires strong justification
If you must skin an interface, hire a professional graphic designer
Windows Media Center exemplifies justified UI deviation due to its unique usage context (remote control from couch)
Usability trumps visual flair - 'boring' standard UI is often the better choice
money_quotes: My eyes! The goggles, they do nothing!
Perhaps boring, but far more usable.
At least have a professional graphic designer put together a skin if you're going to traumatize the user with a non-standard UI.
You better have a damn good reason to deviate from the standard OS look and feel that users expect.
key_insight: Non-standard UI skins need both strong justification and professional execution - without both, you're just traumatizing users for no good reason.
